title: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia: Diagnosis and Management
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of BPH and LUTS
Perform appropriate evaluation using validated tools
Select appropriate medical and surgical therapies

sections.title: Pathophysiology
sections.content:
**What is BPH?**
Benign proliferation of prostatic stromal and epithelial cells in the transition zone, causing bladder outlet obstruction.

**Two Components of Obstruction:**
• **Static:** Physical enlargement of prostate
• **Dynamic:** Smooth muscle tone (α1-receptors)

**Prevalence by Age:**
• 50s: 50%
• 60s: 60%
• 70s: 70%
• 80s: 80%

**Key Concept:** Prostate size does NOT correlate with symptom severity!

sections.title: Clinical Presentation - LUTS
sections.content:
**Storage Symptoms (Irritative):**
• Urgency
• Frequency
• Nocturia
• Urge incontinence

**Voiding Symptoms (Obstructive):**
• Hesitancy
• Weak stream
• Intermittency
• Straining
• Incomplete emptying
• Post-void dribbling

**AUA Symptom Score (IPSS):**
• 0-7: Mild
• 8-19: Moderate
• 20-35: Severe
• Includes quality of life question

sections.title: Evaluation
sections.content:
**History:**
• IPSS/AUA symptom score
• Medical history (DM, neurologic disease)
• Medications (anticholinergics, decongestants)
• Fluid intake patterns

**Physical Exam:**
• DRE: Size, consistency, nodules
• Abdominal exam for bladder distension

**Laboratory:**
• Urinalysis (rule out UTI, hematuria)
• PSA (optional, discuss with patient)
• Creatinine (if retention suspected)

**Post-Void Residual:**
• >200mL suggests significant retention
• Measured by ultrasound or catheterization

sections.title: Medical Therapy
sections.content:
**Alpha-1 Blockers (First-line):**
• Tamsulosin, alfuzosin, silodosin
• Relax prostatic smooth muscle (dynamic component)
• Rapid onset (days to weeks)
• Side effects: Orthostatic hypotension, dizziness, retrograde ejaculation
• Intraoperative Floppy Iris Syndrome (IFIS) - inform ophthalmologist

**5-Alpha Reductase Inhibitors:**
• Finasteride, dutasteride
• Block testosterone→DHT conversion
• Reduce prostate size (static component)
• Slow onset (3-6 months)
• Side effects: Decreased libido, ED, gynecomastia
• Reduces PSA by 50% (adjust interpretation)

**Combination Therapy:**
• Alpha-blocker + 5-ARI for large prostates (>30g)
• More effective than monotherapy
• MTOPS and CombAT trials

sections.title: Other Medical Options
sections.content:
**PDE5 Inhibitors:**
• Tadalafil 5mg daily
• FDA-approved for BPH/LUTS
• Good option if concurrent ED

**Anticholinergics:**
• For predominant storage symptoms
• Use cautiously - can worsen retention
• Monitor PVR

**Beta-3 Agonists:**
• Mirabegron
• Alternative for storage symptoms
• Lower retention risk than anticholinergics

sections.title: Surgical Options
sections.content:
**Indications for Surgery:**
• Refractory urinary retention
• Recurrent UTIs
• Bladder stones
• Renal insufficiency from obstruction
• Recurrent gross hematuria
• Failed medical therapy

**Procedures:**
• **TURP:** Gold standard, prostates 30-80g
• **Laser procedures:** HoLEP, PVP (GreenLight)
• **Simple prostatectomy:** >80-100g prostates
• **Minimally invasive:** UroLift, Rezum, Aquablation

**TURP Syndrome:**
• Hyponatremia from irrigation fluid absorption
• Confusion, nausea, visual changes
• Treat with hypertonic saline

sections.title: Complications of Untreated BPH
sections.content:
**Acute Urinary Retention:**
• Sudden inability to void
• Painful bladder distension
• Treatment: Catheterization, alpha-blocker, trial of void

**Chronic Retention:**
• May be painless
• Overflow incontinence
• Hydronephrosis and renal insufficiency

**Other Complications:**
• Recurrent UTIs
• Bladder stones
• Bladder decompensation
• Gross hematuria

keyTakeaways: BPH has static (size) and dynamic (tone) components
Prostate size does NOT correlate with symptom severity
5-ARIs reduce PSA by 50% - double the value for interpretation
Acute urinary retention is an indication for surgical referral

references.citation: McVary KT et al. Update on AUA Guideline on BPH. J Urol. 2011;185:1793-1803

reviewQuestions.question: A 65-year-old man with BPH on finasteride has a PSA of 2.5 ng/mL. What is his estimated true PSA?
reviewQuestions.explanation: 5-alpha reductase inhibitors reduce PSA by approximately 50%. To interpret PSA in patients on finasteride or dutasteride, double the measured value. His estimated true PSA is 5.0 ng/mL, which warrants further evaluation.

reviewQuestions.question: A 58-year-old man presents with moderate LUTS (IPSS 15) and a prostate estimated at 25g on DRE. Which medication is most appropriate for initial monotherapy?
reviewQuestions.explanation: For a smaller prostate (<30-40g), alpha-blockers alone are first-line therapy. 5-ARIs are most beneficial for larger prostates. Combination therapy is reserved for prostates >30g. Tamsulosin will provide rapid symptom relief.

reviewQuestions.question: Which of the following is an absolute indication for surgical intervention in BPH?
options: Recurrent urinary retention despite medical therapy
reviewQuestions.explanation: Recurrent urinary retention despite medical therapy is an absolute indication for surgery. Other absolute indications include renal insufficiency from obstruction, bladder stones, and recurrent UTIs due to BPH. Symptom severity and prostate size alone are relative indications.
